title: Dyeing with yarrow
subtitle: A clear, warm yellow from yarrow flowers and leaves on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Yarrow (Achillea millefolium) is one of the most reliable garden and hedgerow dye plants in Britain, producing a clear bright yellow to warm gold on alum-mordanted wool from flowers and upper leaves. It grows abundantly in meadows, roadsides, and most gardens, and can be used fresh or dried. This entry covers gathering, mordanting, and dyeing a 100 g skein.
sourceNotes: Yarrow dyeing synthesised from general natural dye references. Achillea millefolium is a well-documented British dye plant producing flavonoid yellows on protein fibres with an alum mordant.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt applied to fibre before dyeing to help the dye bond permanently. Potassium alum is the standard mordant for natural yellow dyes on wool.
The dry weight of the fibre to be dyed, used as the reference for calculating mordant and dye plant quantities. Abbreviated WOF in dye recipes.
The water obtained by simmering yarrow in the pot. The strained liquid forms the bath in which the mordanted wool is dyed.
A small amount of iron solution added to the dye bath or after-bath to shift a yellow dye toward green or olive. Even a tiny addition of iron shifts yarrow yellow to a warm chartreuse.

content.text: Yarrow is one of the most obliging natural dye plants: it is common, grows in most British gardens and roadsides, and produces a strong, reliable yellow from flowers and upper leaves on a standard alum
. The yellow is bright and clear in summer-harvested material and shifts toward warm gold in material gathered as it dries on the plant in late summer. Use 200-300%
of fresh plant material (or 100% dried) for a full, saturated colour. A small addition of an
after dyeing shifts the yellow toward chartreuse or olive.

items.name: Fresh yarrow, flowers and upper leaves

content.text: Dissolve the alum (and cream of tartar if using) in a small amount of hot water and add to the pot with enough water to cover the fibre. Add the pre-wetted skein. Heat slowly to 85°C over 30 minutes. Hold at 85°C for 45 minutes, stirring occasionally. Allow to cool, then lift the skein. The mordanted wool can be used at once or stored damp for up to three days.
Pack the yarrow loosely into the pot with enough water to cover. Bring to a simmer and hold at 85-90°C for 45 minutes. The

will be a clear, deep amber-yellow. Strain the plant material out and return the liquid to the pot. Flowers give a brighter yellow than leaves alone; using the whole upper plant (flowers, leaves, and stems) produces a warmer, slightly more complex colour.

Add the mordanted skein to the strained dye bath. Heat slowly to 80-85°C over 20 minutes. Hold at temperature for 45 minutes, stirring gently every 10 minutes to ensure even colour take-up. Allow the skein to cool in the bath: yarrow continues giving colour as the bath cools and the longer cool period deepens the shade. Rinse the skein in water of the same temperature once the bath is cool to the touch.
Step 4: Dry and record the result

Rinse in two changes of warm water and hang to dry in the shade. Record the date, plant source, plant quantity, mordant percentage, and final colour in a dye journal: yarrow colour varies with season, growing conditions, and the age of the plant material, and records allow you to reproduce a colour in future seasons. Keep a small wound swatch of the finished yarn with the journal entry.

items.problem: The yellow is very pale and flat rather than warm and bright
items.cause: The plant material was gathered too late in the season or the flowers had gone over
items.fix: Gather yarrow when the flowers are fully open but not yet browning at the tips. Increase the plant weight to 300% WOF and prepare a fresh bath.

items.problem: The colour is uneven across the skein with darker and lighter patches
items.cause: The skein was not turned often enough during dyeing, and some sections were shaded from the dye by the weight of yarn above
items.fix: Stir gently every 5-8 minutes and use a skein that is loosely tied to allow dye to penetrate freely. Avoid overcrowding the pot.

items.problem: The colour looks greenish rather than yellow on the dried skein
items.cause: Trace iron from a pot or water source shifted the yellow toward olive
items.fix: Use a stainless steel pot rather than one with any iron or bare metal surfaces. Hard water can also shift yellows slightly; use filtered water if the tap water in your area is particularly hard.
